title: Agent Settings: Timeouts, Idle Messages, Voicemail & Turn-Taking
lede: This is where a good agent becomes a great one. The Settings panel controls everything about how your Smart Agent behaves on a live call — call length, silence handling, voicemail detection, background sound, interruptions, and turn-taking. This guide walks through every section and every switch, A to Z.

steps.title: Open Your Agent
steps.content:
Every setting in this guide lives inside an individual agent, so start by opening one:

  1. In the left sidebar, click Intelligence Center → Smart Agents.
  2. Click the name of the agent you want to tune. The agent editor opens.
💡 Settings are saved per agent — you can give your after-hours agent a short voicemail script and long silence timeout, while your sales agent gets aggressive voicemail hang-up and office background sound.

images.caption: Intelligence Center → Smart Agents — click an agent's name to open its editor.
Intelligence Center → Smart Agents — click an agent's name to open its editor.

steps.title: Open the Settings Panel
steps.content:
The editor has two views (switch anytime with the Form / Node toggle at the top). The Settings panel is the same in both — only the button location differs:

  • Form view: click the Settings button below the prompt editor (next to Sub Agents / Tasks / Handoffs and Tools / MCP).
  • Node view: click the Settings card (labeled Configure) in the capabilities bar along the top of the canvas.

A Settings window opens with a section rail on the left: General, Idle Messages, Voicemail, Speech, Voice Activity Detection, Audio Quality, Background Audio, Interruptions, User Turn Limits, Turn Detection, and Privacy & Recording.

💡 Changes save automatically as you adjust them — the Done button simply closes the window.

images.caption: Form view: the Settings button sits below the prompt editor.
Form view: the Settings button sits below the prompt editor.

steps.title: General — Call Length and Silence
steps.content:
The General section opens first. It holds the two hard limits of every call:

  • Maximum Duration — the longest a call can run, from 10 to 3,600 seconds (1 hour). When the limit is reached, the agent ends the call.
  • Silence Timeout — how many seconds of total inactivity (10–600) before the call is automatically ended.
⚠️ Silence Timeout actually hangs up the call. If you just want the agent to say "Are you still there?" when the caller goes quiet, use Idle Messages (next step) with a shorter timeout than this one.

For transcription-only agents (LLM and Voice off, Transcriber on), an extra Transcribe each party separately toggle appears here — it runs one speaker-tagged transcriber per caller so human-to-human calls transcribe both sides correctly.

images.caption: The Settings panel: section rail on the left, Maximum Duration and Silence Timeout in General.
The Settings panel: section rail on the left, Maximum Duration and Silence Timeout in General.

steps.title: Idle Messages — Re-Engage a Quiet Caller
steps.content:
Click Idle Messages in the left rail. This is your agent's polite nudge when the caller stops responding:

  1. Keep the Idle Messages toggle ON to enable the feature.
  2. In Idle Messages, pick one or more prompts (e.g. "Hey, are you still there?"). To add your own, type it in the Add Custom Message and Press Enter box — type { to insert a dynamic variable such as the caller's name.
  3. Max Idle Messages (1–10) — how many times the agent may speak up during one call before giving up.
  4. Idle Timeout (5–15 seconds) — how long the caller must be quiet before an idle message is spoken.

Below that, User Presence → User-Away Timeout is a separate, SDK-level detector: after this much silence (default 15s) it fires a "user away" signal so the agent can react before the hard Silence Timeout hangs up. Toggle it off to disable.

images.caption: Idle Messages: prompts, max count, idle timeout, and the User-Away Timeout.
Idle Messages: prompts, max count, idle timeout, and the User-Away Timeout.

steps.title: Voicemail — Detect Answering Machines (AMD)
steps.content:
Click Voicemail in the left rail. On outbound calls, Answering Machine Detection classifies who (or what) answered — human, voicemail, IVR, or unavailable — so your agent never wastes a pitch on a beep:

  1. Turn Voicemail Detection (AMD) ON.
  2. Choose what happens on a machine: Hang up if reach voicemail or Leave a message if reach voicemail.
  3. If you chose to leave a message, type it in the message box — use {{}} to insert dynamic variables. Keep it under 200 characters; anything longer is cut off.

Detection Tuning (Advanced) — leave at defaults unless calls are being mis-classified:

  • No-Speech Timeout (default 10s) — how long AMD waits for any audio after the call answers.
  • Detection Timeout (default 20s) — hard ceiling for the whole detection; must be ≥ No-Speech Timeout.
  • Human Speech Threshold (default 2.5s) — greetings shorter than this are treated as human; longer ones go to the classifier.
  • Human Silence Threshold (default 0.5s) — silence after a short greeting before committing to "human".
  • Machine Silence Threshold (default 1.5s) — silence after machine-like speech before a verdict.
  • Interrupt Agent on Machine — stop the agent mid-sentence the moment a machine is detected (recommended ON).
  • Wait for Detection Before Speaking — hold the opening greeting until AMD decides, so it never plays into a voicemail.
  • Don't Reply Until Detection Finishes — for "caller speaks first" agents, hold the first reply until classification completes.
  • Classifier Hint Phrases (up to 50) — add domain-specific greetings (Spanish carriers, branded voicemail, etc.) as extra examples to bias the classifier.
⚠️ If your account-level Override My Agents voicemail setting is on (Call Center → Voicemail Settings), it supersedes this agent's voicemail behavior — a blue banner at the top of this section will tell you.

images.caption: Voicemail Detection (AMD) enabled, with hang-up/leave-message modes and advanced detection tuning.
Voicemail Detection (AMD) enabled, with hang-up/leave-message modes and advanced detection tuning.

steps.title: Speech — Backchannel, Latency & Pacing
steps.content:
Click Speech in the left rail. These settings make the agent sound faster and more human:

  • Enable Backchannel (Beta) — the agent drops natural affirmations like "yeah" and "uh-huh" while the caller talks. When ON you can tune Backchannel Frequency (Less ↔ More) and pick the Backchannel Words it may use (add your own and press Enter).
  • Preemptive Generation — the agent starts thinking (LLM) before the caller fully finishes speaking, cutting response latency. When ON you also get:
    • Preemptive TTS — generate audio speculatively too. Even lower latency, slightly higher cost (discarded audio still bills).
    • Skip Preemptive If User Speaks Longer Than (default 10s) — very long utterances are too unstable to speculate on.
    • Max Preemptive Retries (default 3) — how many times to re-speculate per turn.

Pacing & Echo:

  • AEC Warmup Duration (default 3s) — suppresses interruptions while echo cancellation settles on the opening greeting, so the agent's own voice doesn't trigger false interrupts.
  • Min Consecutive Speech Delay (default 0s) — a forced pause between the agent's own back-to-back turns; 0.3–1.0s gives a more natural cadence.
  • Pre-TTS Text Cleanup — transforms applied to agent text before it's spoken: filter_markdown strips ** _ # symbols, filter_emoji drops emoji. You can add custom transform names your TTS provider recognizes.

images.caption: Speech: backchannel, preemptive generation, AEC warmup, pacing, and pre-TTS text cleanup.
Speech: backchannel, preemptive generation, AEC warmup, pacing, and pre-TTS text cleanup.

steps.title: Voice Activity Detection — Hearing the Caller
steps.content:
Click Voice Activity Detection in the left rail. VAD decides when the caller is speaking versus silent — the foundation of natural turn-taking:

  • Activation Threshold (default 0.5) — sensitivity of speech detection. Lower catches softer voices but may pick up background noise; higher reduces false positives but can miss quiet speakers.
  • Min Speech Duration (default 0.05s) — minimum continuous speech to register a new speech chunk.
  • Min Silence Duration (default 0.55s) — how long to wait after speech ends before deciding the caller finished.
  • Prefix Padding (default 0.5s) — audio prepended to each detected chunk so the start of words isn't clipped.
  • Max Buffered Speech (default 60s) — the most speech kept in the VAD buffer.
💡 Calls from noisy environments (job sites, cars) triggering ghost interruptions? Nudge the Activation Threshold up toward 0.6–0.7 before touching anything else.

images.caption: VAD: activation threshold, speech/silence durations, prefix padding, and buffer size.
VAD: activation threshold, speech/silence durations, prefix padding, and buffer size.

steps.title: Audio Quality — Microphone-Side Processing
steps.content:
Click Audio Quality in the left rail. This audio processing is applied before the platform listens (VAD / transcription):

  • Auto-Gain Control (AGC) — normalizes input loudness so quiet callers come through louder. Leave it ON for phone calls; turn it off only for professional/studio audio setups where you want to preserve dynamics.

images.caption: Audio Quality: the Auto-Gain Control toggle.
Audio Quality: the Auto-Gain Control toggle.

steps.title: Background Audio — Make the Agent Feel Human
steps.content:
Click Background Audio in the left rail. This is one of the platform's best-kept secrets: a continuous ambient track behind the agent's voice makes it feel like a real person in a real office — and a soft typing sound fills the silence while the AI is thinking:

  1. Turn Enable Background Audio ON (master toggle — when off, no extra audio is published).
  2. Ambient Sound — toggle the Clip on and pick a loop that plays for the whole call: Office ambience (great B2B default), City ambience, Forest ambience, Crowded room, or Hold music. Set its Volume (default 0.8).
  3. Thinking Sound — toggle the Clip on and pick a short sound that plays between the caller finishing and the agent replying. The Keyboard typing clips are made for this. Set its Volume too.

All clips are bundled with the platform — nothing to upload.

images.caption: Background Audio enabled with an office-ambience loop and a keyboard-typing thinking sound.
Background Audio enabled with an office-ambience loop and a keyboard-typing thinking sound.

steps.title: Interruptions — Letting Callers Cut In
steps.content:
Click Interruptions in the left rail. Real people interrupt — this section controls how gracefully your agent handles it:

  • Allow Interruptions — lets the caller cut the agent off mid-sentence. (Ignored when using a realtime speech-to-speech model with built-in turn detection.)
  • Interruption Mode:
    • VAD only (default) — interrupts on any detected voice activity. Lowest cost, most aggressive.
    • Adaptive (ML) — a smarter model decides whether the caller really means to interrupt. Not available with OpenAI Realtime.
    • Auto — let the platform pick.
  • Min Interruption Duration (default 0.5s) — minimum detected speech before an interruption triggers.
  • Min Interruption Words (default 0) — minimum recognized words to count as an interruption (0 = duration only). Raising this to 2–3 stops coughs and "uh-huh"s from cutting the agent off.

False Interruption Handling:

  • Resume After False Interruption — if the "interruption" produced no recognizable words (a noise), the agent resumes from where it left off.
  • False Interruption Timeout (default 2s) — how long to wait before ruling it a false interruption. Toggle Disable handling to treat every interruption as intentional.

When Adaptive mode is selected, an extra Backchannel Boundary control appears — it suppresses interruptions near turn boundaries (SDK default 1.0s at turn start, 3.5s at turn end).

images.caption: Interruptions: allow toggle, mode, duration/word thresholds, and false-interruption handling.
Interruptions: allow toggle, mode, duration/word thresholds, and false-interruption handling.

steps.title: User Turn Limits — Tame Runaway Monologues
steps.content:
Click User Turn Limits in the left rail. Some callers never stop talking — either limit forces the agent to end the caller's turn and respond. Both off = unlimited (the platform default):

  • Max Words Per Turn — force-end the caller's turn after this many recognized words. Suggested 100–300 for chatty domains.
  • Max Duration Per Turn — force-end after this much continuous speech. Suggested 30–60 seconds for screening flows.

images.caption: User Turn Limits: word and duration caps for a single caller turn.
User Turn Limits: word and duration caps for a single caller turn.

steps.title: Turn Detection — When to Listen, When to Speak
steps.content:
Click Turn Detection in the left rail. This decides when a caller's turn begins and ends. (Realtime speech-to-speech models ignore these settings — they handle turns themselves.)

  • Turn Detector Enabled — uses an audio-based model that listens to intonation, pitch, and rhythm directly (no transcript needed). When off, the agent falls back to VAD-only or transcriber endpointing.
  • Override End-of-Turn Sensitivity — the detector ships with calibrated per-language defaults; turn this on only to override how confident it must be before ending a turn (English default ≈ 0.36; lower = faster responses, higher = more patient).
  • Endpointing Mode:
    • Fixed (default) — always waits the min/max delays below. Predictable.
    • Dynamic — adapts the end-of-turn delay to the conversation's pace: snappier with fast talkers, more patient with slow ones. Exposes a Smoothing α slider (default 0.9 — higher adapts more slowly).
  • Min Endpointing Delay (default 0.5s) — wait before considering the turn complete when no detector signal is available.
  • Max Endpointing Delay (default 6s) — maximum wait when the detector believes the caller will keep talking.
💡 Agent talking over callers who pause mid-thought? Raise Min Endpointing Delay to 0.8–1.0s. Agent feels sluggish? Lower it — or switch Endpointing Mode to Dynamic.

images.caption: Turn Detection: audio-based detector, sensitivity override, and endpointing delays.
Turn Detection: audio-based detector, sensitivity override, and endpointing delays.

steps.title: Privacy & Recording, Then You're Done
steps.content:
Finally, click Privacy & Recording in the left rail:

  • Disable Sensitive Data Storage — when ON, iMash will not store or log the call transcript or recording for this agent. (This also disables the Audio Recording toggle below.)
  • Audio Recording — record conversations with this agent so you can play them back from Call Logs.
  1. Click Done to close the panel — everything you changed is already saved.
  2. Hit Test Agent in the editor header and have a conversation. You'll hear your background audio, feel the new turn-taking, and can interrupt the agent to check your interruption settings.

That's it — your agent now handles silence, voicemail, and chatty callers exactly the way you designed. 🎉

⚠️ If your agent uses OpenAI Realtime as its LLM, a warning banner appears in Settings: some interruption and turn-detection options are handled by the realtime model itself and will be ignored.

images.caption: Privacy & Recording: sensitive-data storage opt-out and the audio-recording toggle.
Privacy & Recording: sensitive-data storage opt-out and the audio-recording toggle.

support.title: Need Help?
support.content:
If you run into any issues or have questions:

  • 📧 Email us at support@imash.io
  • 💬 Join our Discord community
  • 📚 Check out our API Reference
  • 🎥 Watch our video tutorials
